phases.name: Phase 1
phases.route: London Euston to Birmingham Curzon Street

features.name: Euston Station
features.description: London terminus for HS2. Originally designed for 11 platforms as an integrated through-station, the scope was reduced to 6 underground platforms in a 2023 redesign to lower costs. Enabling works and utility diversions have been under way since 2017, but main construction has not yet started on the revised design.
features.costNote: Initial budget ~£2.6bn before 2023 redesign. NAO estimate for the station element: £4.8bn (2019 prices), approximately £6bn in current prices. Combined Euston tunnels and station: ~£9.4bn in current prices.
features.costDriver: Repeated redesigns. The original 11-platform through-station was replaced with a 6-platform underground terminus, but the redesign process itself consumed years of professional fees and abortive construction work.

features.name: Euston Utility Diversions
features.description: Relocation of gas mains, water pipes, electricity cables, sewers, and telecoms beneath some of central London's busiest streets to clear the Euston construction zone. Work has required multiple road closures and coordination with dozens of asset owners.
features.costDriver: Central London subsurface congestion. Moving utilities beneath dense urban streets is slow, expensive, and requires coordination with many asset owners, each with veto power over timelines.

features.name: St James's Gardens Burial Ground
features.description: Archaeological excavation and respectful removal of approximately 45,000 burials from a disused burial ground adjacent to Euston station. One of the largest archaeological projects in UK history, requiring individual exhumation, osteological analysis, and reburial.
features.costDriver: Scale was underestimated. Initial surveys suggested far fewer burials. The actual count of ~45,000 made this one of the largest archaeological exhumation programmes ever conducted in the UK.

features.name: Old Oak Common Station
features.description: Super-hub interchange station in west London connecting HS2 with the Elizabeth Line, Great Western Main Line, and Heathrow services. Currently serving as the planned interim London terminus while Euston is redesigned. The station box excavation is one of the largest in Europe.
features.costNote: Contract awarded at ~£1bn in 2019. Cost rose to £1.67bn (2019 prices) by 2021, approximately £2.1bn in current prices.
features.costDriver: Scope expanded to serve as interim terminus (originally a through-station only), adding complexity. The station box at 850m is one of the largest excavations in Europe, and integration with three existing rail lines requires complex interfaces.

features.name: Northolt Tunnel
features.description: Twin-bore tunnel running 13.5 km under the suburbs of northwest London from Old Oak Common to the Colne Valley. Bored by two tunnel boring machines (TBMs), 'Sushila' and 'Caroline', both of which completed their drives in 2024.
features.costNote: Included in the SCS JV contract (awarded £3.3bn, risen to £4.1bn in 2019 prices / ~£5.1bn current), which also covers Euston tunnels and approaches.
features.costDriver: Tunnelling beneath a major city is inherently expensive, requiring ground treatment, settlement monitoring of thousands of properties, and cross-passages every 500m for emergency egress.

features.name: Northolt Tunnel Vent Shafts and Headhouses
features.description: Five intermediate shafts along the Northolt Tunnel providing ventilation, emergency access, and pressure relief for high-speed trains. Each shaft is 15-25m deep with a surface headhouse building designed to blend into the suburban streetscape.
features.costDriver: Each shaft requires its own construction compound, deep excavation in variable ground conditions, and a surface building subject to local planning requirements.

features.name: Colne Valley Viaduct
features.description: At 3.4 km, the longest railway bridge in the United Kingdom. Crosses the Colne Valley regional park, a complex of reservoirs, lakes, waterways, and ecological habitats northwest of London. The deck is launched incrementally from a casting yard at the north end.
features.contractor: Align JV (Bouygues, VolkerFitzpatrick, Sir Robert McAlpine)
features.costNote: Part of the Align JV contract (£1.6bn at NtP 2020, ~£2bn in current prices), which also covers the Chiltern Tunnel.
features.costDriver: The viaduct must cross a complex aquatic landscape with minimal disruption to watercourses, lakes, and habitats. The incremental launch method requires a bespoke casting facility and each of the 1,000+ segments is individually manufactured.

features.name: Colne Valley Ecological Mitigation
features.description: Comprehensive ecological mitigation for the Colne Valley crossing, including creation of replacement aquatic habitats, translocation of protected species (great crested newts, water voles, bats), and construction of wildlife crossings. Includes a dedicated wildlife bridge and extensive planting.
features.costDriver: The Colne Valley is a biodiversity hotspot. Mitigation must replace like-for-like habitats for multiple protected species under licence, and must be maintained for decades. Licence conditions require habitats to be established before construction impacts, adding years to the programme.

features.name: Chiltern Tunnel
features.description: 16 km twin-bore tunnel under the Chilterns Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty (AONB), the longest on the route. Bored by TBMs 'Florence' and 'Cecilia'. Both machines completed their drives in 2024, making this the longest tunnel bored in the UK.
features.costNote: Part of the Align JV contract (£1.6bn at NtP 2020, ~£2bn in current prices). The Chiltern Tunnel is the single most expensive structure on the route.
features.costDriver: The tunnel was mandated to protect the AONB landscape. At 16 km twin-bore (32 km total boring), it is the single most expensive element of the route, equivalent in length to the Channel Tunnel's undersea section.

features.name: Chilterns AONB Landscape Mitigation
features.description: Landscape and environmental mitigation across the Chilterns AONB, including translocation of ancient woodland soils and flora from Mantles Wood and Jones Hill Wood, chalk grassland restoration, and visual screening planting. Jones Hill Wood became a public campaign focus.
features.costDriver: Ancient woodland translocation is experimental and expensive. Each woodland requires topsoil, seed bank, and mature trees to be relocated with no guarantee of successful establishment. Maintenance obligations extend decades beyond construction.

features.name: Small Dean Viaduct
features.description: Viaduct near Wendover in Buckinghamshire crossing the A413, the Chiltern railway line, and a flood plain. Carries the high-speed line across the valley on a series of concrete spans.
features.costDriver: Crossing an existing railway and major road in a flood plain required complex construction phasing to avoid disrupting live traffic and rail services.

features.name: Wendover Area Noise Mitigation
features.description: Extensive noise barriers, earth bunds, and cutting deepening through the Wendover area in response to community campaigns. The route passes within 200m of residential areas. Mitigation includes 4m+ noise barriers, landscaped bunds, and a community fund.
features.costDriver: Community opposition led to route deepening, taller barriers, and a community fund. Each design iteration added cost without changing the route alignment.

features.name: A413 Realignment
features.description: Realignment of a section of the A413 road near Wendover to accommodate the HS2 alignment. Includes new junctions, road widening, and integration with the Small Dean Viaduct crossing.
features.costDriver: Road diversions require full design to modern standards (drainage, lighting, junctions) and must maintain traffic flow during construction.

features.name: Stoke Mandeville Realignment
features.description: Realignment of the B4443 and associated infrastructure near Stoke Mandeville to avoid the Stoke Mandeville Stadium, home of the Paralympic movement. The route was adjusted to increase separation from the stadium grounds.
features.costDriver: Route adjustment to protect a nationally significant heritage site added engineering complexity and additional land take.

features.name: Calvert Infrastructure Maintenance Depot
features.description: Purpose-built depot near Calvert for the maintenance of track, signalling, and overhead line equipment. The depot will house maintenance trains and store materials for the entire Phase 1 route.
features.costDriver: A 50-hectare greenfield depot with bespoke rail-connected buildings, fuel storage, and environmental containment. Not visible to passengers but essential for operations.

features.name: Calvert to Brackley Archaeological Programme
features.description: Large-scale archaeological investigations between Calvert and Brackley uncovered extensive Roman, medieval, and Iron Age remains including a Roman town, villa complexes, and an Anglo-Saxon settlement. Over 40 archaeological sites were excavated.
features.contractor: EKFB JV / MOLA (Museum of London Archaeology)
features.costDriver: The volume and significance of archaeological discoveries far exceeded expectations. Each find required professional excavation, recording, and analysis before construction could proceed, causing repeated localised delays.

features.name: Greatworth Green Tunnel
features.description: A 'green tunnel' (cut-and-cover) structure designed to reduce noise and visual impact on the surrounding countryside. The roof is landscaped with soil and planting to blend into the environment.
features.contractor: EKFB JV (Eiffage, Kier, Ferrovial, BAM Nuttall)
features.costDriver: Green tunnels are significantly more expensive than open cuttings but were required by the planning process to reduce visual impact in open countryside.

features.name: Chipping Warden Green Tunnel
features.description: A 'green tunnel' (cut-and-cover) near Chipping Warden. Like Greatworth, it was added during the planning process to reduce the visual and noise impact of the line passing through open countryside.
features.costDriver: Added as a planning concession. Cut-and-cover structures require full excavation, concrete box construction, waterproofing, backfill, and landscaping, all far more costly than surface running.

features.name: HS2/WCML Delta Junction
features.description: A complex junction where HS2 connects to the existing West Coast Main Line, allowing trains to transfer between the two routes. The delta junction involves multiple flyovers, diving structures, and signalling interfaces to enable seamless running.
features.costDriver: Integrating a new 360 km/h railway with a Victorian-era main line requires complex civil engineering and signalling that must not disrupt existing services during construction.

features.name: Long Itchington Wood Tunnel
features.description: A 1.6 km bored tunnel passing beneath Long Itchington Wood, an ancient woodland and Site of Special Scientific Interest (SSSI). TBM 'Dorothy' completed the first bore in 2023, and 'Eunice' completed the second in 2024.
features.costDriver: Tunnelling was chosen over a surface route to protect an ancient woodland SSSI. A tunnel for 1.6 km of route costs vastly more than a cutting or embankment, but was non-negotiable under environmental law.

features.name: Ancient Woodland Translocation Programme
features.description: HS2 affects 36 ancient woodlands along the Phase 1 route. Mitigation includes soil and flora translocation, new planting of 7 million trees and shrubs, and long-term habitat management plans extending 30+ years. Ancient woodland is classified as irreplaceable habitat under planning policy.
features.costDriver: Planning policy classifies ancient woodland as irreplaceable. Mitigation requires decades of soil translocation, replanting, and management. Natural England licence conditions are strict and non-negotiable.

features.name: Burton Green Tunnel
features.description: A 700m green tunnel (cut-and-cover) through the village of Burton Green near Kenilworth. Added to the design to reduce noise and severance impacts on the community after a sustained local campaign.
features.costDriver: Originally the route was to run in an open cutting here. The green tunnel was added as a community mitigation, with the additional cost borne by the programme.

features.name: Kenilworth Greenway Diversion
features.description: Diversion of the Kenilworth Greenway, a historic walking and cycling route built on a former railway line. The original greenway is severed by the HS2 alignment and must be rerouted via a new bridge and path.
features.costDriver: Severing an established public right of way requires replacement infrastructure to equivalent or better standard, including a new bridge.

features.name: River Blythe Viaduct and Diversion
features.description: Crossing of the River Blythe flood plain, including a viaduct and realignment of a section of the river. The River Blythe is a SSSI, and the crossing requires extensive environmental mitigation including new wetland habitat creation.
features.costDriver: Crossing a SSSI river requires environmental mitigation far exceeding the cost of the bridge itself: channel realignment, flood compensation, aquatic habitat creation, and multi-year monitoring.

features.name: Birmingham Interchange Station
features.description: Station adjacent to the National Exhibition Centre (NEC) and Birmingham Airport, providing interchange with the wider West Midlands transport network. Designed to serve the eastern side of the conurbation and the planned UK Central development.
features.costNote: Original budget £270m. Contract awarded July 2022 at £370m (2019 prices), approximately £440m in current prices.
features.costDriver: An additional station (beyond Curzon Street) adds substantial cost: platforms, concourse, car park for 7,400 vehicles, people mover, and road access modifications to the M42 junction.

features.name: M42 Junction 6 Upgrade
features.description: Major reconfiguration of M42 Junction 6 to provide road access to the new Birmingham Interchange station and accommodate increased traffic. Involves new slip roads, a realigned roundabout, and modifications to the existing motorway bridges.
features.costDriver: Motorway junction upgrades are among the most expensive road works, requiring construction alongside live traffic, temporary traffic management, and coordination with National Highways.

features.name: Bat Mitigation Programme
features.description: Route-wide programme to mitigate impacts on bat populations, including construction of purpose-built bat barns and hibernacula, installation of bat bridges and hop-overs, and modification of tunnel portals to allow bat passage. Multiple species are affected including Bechstein's bat, a rare species found in the Chilterns.
features.costDriver: Bat protection is legally mandated under the Wildlife and Countryside Act and the Conservation of Habitats Regulations. Each colony requires survey, a bespoke mitigation plan, and years of post-construction monitoring.

features.name: Bromford Tunnel
features.description: A 5.6 km twin-bore tunnel carrying HS2 beneath the northeast suburbs of Birmingham into the city centre. TBMs for this section were launched in 2023.
features.costNote: Part of the BBV JV contract (£4.8bn at NtP 2020, ~£6bn in current prices), covering all works from Long Itchington Wood to Birmingham.
features.costDriver: Urban tunnelling beneath residential areas of Birmingham, with settlement monitoring, utility protection, and construction logistics constrained by narrow suburban streets.

features.name: Washwood Heath Rolling Stock Depot
features.description: The main depot for HS2 train maintenance, stabling, and cleaning. Located on a former Metropolitan-Cammell railway works site. The depot will house the full fleet of high-speed trains with heavy maintenance, cleaning, and fuelling facilities.
features.costDriver: A full-service depot for a high-speed fleet requires bespoke buildings, rail-connected facilities, and environmental containment. The site required extensive remediation of historic industrial contamination.

features.name: Birmingham Curzon Street Station
features.description: The Birmingham city centre terminus for HS2, located on the site of the original Curzon Street station (1838). The design by WSP/Grimshaw features a triple-arched roof. Construction of the station itself has not yet started; enabling works and demolition are largely complete.
features.contractor: Mace Dragados JV (enabling), main works TBC
features.costNote: Construction partner contract awarded May 2021 at £570m, approximately £710m in current prices.
features.costDriver: City centre station construction on a constrained site adjacent to listed buildings, with complex ground conditions and integration with the existing Moor Street station.

features.name: ETCS Signalling and Overhead Line Equipment
features.description: European Train Control System Level 2 signalling for the full route, plus 25 kV overhead line equipment (OLE) powered from new National Grid connections. ETCS is a digital signalling system that communicates directly with trains, eliminating lineside signals.
features.costNote: Combined rail systems contracts awarded November 2024 at ~£3bn (near-current prices).
features.costDriver: ETCS deployment in the UK has no precedent at this scale. Procurement has been delayed by the need to integrate with legacy British signalling at junctions with the conventional network.

features.name: Rolling Stock (Train Fleet)
features.description: The high-speed train fleet for HS2 services. The rolling stock contract was originally expected to be let by 2020 but has been repeatedly delayed as the scope of the railway has changed. The specification calls for 360 km/h capability, but this may be reviewed.
features.costNote: Contract awarded December 2021 at £1.97bn for 54 trains plus 12 years of maintenance, approximately £2.3bn in current prices.
features.costDriver: The rolling stock contract was originally expected by 2020 but was delayed until December 2021 by uncertainty over route scope. The first trains are not expected until the early 2030s.

costTimeline.event: Initial DfT estimate for full Y-network

spending.note: Cumulative spend to February 2026, from HS2 six-monthly report to Parliament (March 2026). All figures in billions, 2019 prices unless stated.
spending.fundingNote: HS2 Ltd and the DfT agree the £44.6bn funding envelope will not be sufficient to complete Phase 1. An updated cost estimate has been promised as part of the 'HS2 reset' but has not yet been published.

contracts.name: SCS JV (Skanska, Costain, STRABAG)
contracts.scope: Lots S1 & S2: Euston tunnels and approaches, Northolt Tunnel

contracts.name: Align JV (Bouygues, VolkerFitzpatrick, Sir Robert McAlpine)
contracts.scope: Lot C1: Northolt Tunnel to Chiltern Tunnel south portal, Colne Valley Viaduct, Chiltern Tunnel

contracts.name: EKFB JV (Eiffage, Kier, Ferrovial, BAM Nuttall)
contracts.scope: Lots C2 & C3: Chiltern Tunnel north portal to Long Itchington Wood

contracts.name: BBV JV (Balfour Beatty, VINCI)
contracts.scope: Lots N1 & N2: Long Itchington Wood to WCML delta junction and Birmingham

contracts.name: Hitachi-Alstom JV
contracts.scope: 54 high-speed trains plus 12 years of maintenance

contracts.name: Multiple (Ferrovial/BAM, Siemens, Hitachi/Telent)
contracts.scope: Track, ETCS signalling, operations telecoms, communications

contracts.name: CS JV, Fusion JV, LM JV
contracts.scope: Enabling works across all three areas (South, Central, North)

sources.name: National Audit Office, HS2 Progress Update, July 2024
House of Commons Library, HS2 Briefing Paper
